quiz_questions.question_text: What is crystallization?

options.text: A process where a dissolved substance forms solid crystals from a solution
A process where a solid is broken down into a liquid by applying strong heat
A process where two liquids are separated based on their different densities
A process where a gas is converted directly into a solid by strong compression

quiz_questions.explanation: Crystallization is the process where dissolved solute particles come together and form an organized solid structure (crystals) as the solution cools or the solvent evaporates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is filtration?

options.text: A method of heating a liquid until it completely evaporates
A method of separating a solid from a liquid using a filter
A method of cooling a gas until it becomes a liquid substance
A method of dissolving a solid in water to make a clear solution

quiz_questions.explanation: Filtration is a separation technique that uses a porous barrier (like filter paper) to trap solid particles while allowing the liquid to pass through.

quiz_questions.question_text: What must happen to a solution before crystals can form?

options.text: It must become unsaturated by adding more solvent to the mix
It must become saturated or supersaturated with dissolved solute
It must be heated to its boiling point and kept there for an hour
It must be placed under high pressure in a sealed metal container

quiz_questions.explanation: For crystals to form, the solution must become saturated (holding the maximum dissolved solute) or supersaturated (holding more than normal). Cooling or evaporation causes excess solute to crystallize out.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the solid material left on the filter paper after filtration called?

options.text: The filtrate
The distillate
The residue
The solvent

quiz_questions.explanation: The solid trapped by the filter paper is called the residue. The liquid that passes through is called the filtrate.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does cooling a hot saturated solution cause crystals to form?

options.text: Cold water can hold more dissolved substance, so crystals dissolve
Cold water holds less dissolved substance, so excess comes out as crystals
The cold temperature freezes the water and traps the solute in ice crystals
Cooling causes a chemical reaction that converts the solute into solid crystals

quiz_questions.explanation: Most substances are less soluble in cold water than hot water. When a hot saturated solution cools, it can no longer hold as much dissolved solute, so the excess forms solid crystals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What common item is used as a filter in a basic filtration setup?

options.text: Aluminum foil
Plastic wrap
Filter paper
Wax paper

quiz_questions.explanation: Filter paper is a porous paper specially designed to allow liquids to pass through while trapping solid particles. It is placed in a funnel during the filtration process.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of mixture can filtration separate?

options.text: A mixture of two miscible liquids blended together
A mixture of an insoluble solid and a liquid together
A mixture of two different gases mixed in a container
A mixture of dissolved salt and water in a beaker now

quiz_questions.explanation: Filtration works best for separating an insoluble solid from a liquid. The solid particles are too large to pass through the filter paper, while the liquid flows through.

quiz_questions.question_text: Making rock candy by growing sugar crystals on a string is an example of which process?

options.text: Filtration
Distillation
Crystallization
Chromatography

quiz_questions.explanation: Rock candy is made by dissolving sugar in hot water to create a supersaturated solution. As the solution slowly cools and water evaporates, sugar crystals grow on the string.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does a funnel hold the filter paper during filtration?

options.text: The funnel adds chemicals that help separate the solid from the liquid
The funnel supports the paper and directs the filtrate into a container
The funnel heats the mixture so the liquid evaporates more efficiently
The funnel makes the solid particles smaller so they pass through the paper

quiz_questions.explanation: The funnel provides physical support for the filter paper and channels the liquid filtrate downward into the collection container below.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which factor most affects the size of crystals that form during crystallization?

options.text: The color of the solute being crystallized from the solution
The speed at which the solution cools down during the process
The type of container used to hold the solution while cooling
The room lighting present during the crystallization experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: Slow cooling allows more time for solute particles to arrange in an orderly pattern, producing larger crystals. Rapid cooling creates many small crystals because particles have less time to organize.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a saturated solution?

options.text: A solution that contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature
A solution that has no solute dissolved in it and is made only of pure solvent liquid
A solution that has been heated to its boiling point and is actively producing vapor
A solution that contains two or more different solvents mixed together in one container

quiz_questions.explanation: A saturated solution holds the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a specific temperature. Adding more solute will cause it to remain undissolved at the bottom.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a supersaturated solution?

options.text: A solution containing more dissolved solute than it normally can hold at that temperature
A solution that has been diluted with extra water until it is very weak in concentration
A solution that has been frozen solid and no longer contains any liquid solvent at all
A solution that contains exactly half the maximum amount of solute it could possibly hold

quiz_questions.explanation: A supersaturated solution contains more dissolved solute than is normally possible at that temperature. It is unstable and can crystallize suddenly when disturbed or when a seed crystal is added.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'solute' mean?

options.text: The substance that is dissolved in a solution
The liquid that does the dissolving in a solution
The solid left behind after filtration is complete
The gas produced when a solution is heated up high

quiz_questions.explanation: The solute is the substance that dissolves in a solvent to form a solution. For example, in saltwater, salt is the solute and water is the solvent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'insoluble' mean?

options.text: Able to dissolve easily in a particular solvent to form a clear solution
Unable to dissolve in a particular solvent and remaining as a separate solid
Able to melt when heated to a temperature above its normal melting point
Unable to be filtered because the particles are too small for filter paper

quiz_questions.explanation: An insoluble substance does not dissolve in a given solvent. Sand is insoluble in water, which is why filtration can separate sand from water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'seed crystal'?

options.text: A small crystal added to a solution to encourage more crystals to grow around it
The first liquid drop that passes through filter paper during a filtration process
A type of mineral found in soil that plants use as nutrients for their growth
A chemical substance added to a solution to help dissolve more solute in the mix

quiz_questions.explanation: A seed crystal is a small crystal introduced into a supersaturated solution to trigger crystallization. Dissolved solute particles arrange themselves around the seed, growing larger crystals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'solubility' mean?

options.text: The maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a given amount of solvent at a specific temperature
The speed at which a solid substance dissolves when placed into a liquid solvent at room temperature
The ability of a filter to trap solid particles and prevent them from passing through the paper
The temperature at which a substance changes from a solid state into a liquid state of matter

quiz_questions.explanation: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a specific amount of solvent at a given temperature. It usually increases with temperature for solid solutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'filtrate' mean?

options.text: The solid material that remains trapped on the filter paper surface
The liquid that passes through the filter during the filtration process
The funnel that holds the filter paper during the filtration experiment
The crystals that form when a saturated solution is cooled down slowly

quiz_questions.explanation: The filtrate is the liquid that successfully passes through the filter paper during filtration. It should be free of the insoluble solid particles that were trapped.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'residue' mean in filtration?

options.text: The clear liquid collected beneath the filter funnel in the flask
The filter paper itself that is used in the separation procedure
The solid material left behind on the filter after the liquid passes
The solvent used to dissolve the solute before performing filtration

quiz_questions.explanation: The residue is the solid that remains on the filter paper after filtration. It consists of the insoluble particles that were too large to pass through the pores.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'crystal lattice' mean?

options.text: The repeating, orderly three-dimensional arrangement of particles in a crystal
The random, disordered arrangement of particles found in a liquid substance
The type of filter paper used in laboratory filtration experiments conducted
The container used to collect the filtrate after it passes through the filter

quiz_questions.explanation: A crystal lattice is the highly organized, repeating three-dimensional pattern of atoms, ions, or molecules in a crystal. This ordered structure gives crystals their regular geometric shapes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'recrystallization' mean?

options.text: Dissolving impure crystals in hot solvent and then crystallizing again to get purer crystals
Breaking crystals apart into smaller pieces using a mortar and pestle in the chemistry lab
Melting crystals completely so they turn into a liquid and lose their crystal shape forever
Mixing different types of crystals together to form a new compound with new properties

quiz_questions.explanation: Recrystallization is a purification technique where impure crystals are dissolved in hot solvent, filtered to remove insoluble impurities, then cooled to form purer crystals.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wants to recover pure salt from a mixture of salt and sand. Which combination of techniques should they use?

options.text: Dissolve in water, filter to remove sand, then evaporate water to crystallize salt
Heat the mixture until both salt and sand melt, then pour them into separate jars
Use a magnet to attract the salt particles and leave the sand behind on the table
Use chromatography paper to separate the salt from the sand by capillary action

quiz_questions.explanation: Add water to dissolve the salt (sand is insoluble), filter to remove the sand, then heat the saltwater to evaporate the water and crystallize the pure salt.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves a large amount of sugar in hot water and then lets the solution cool slowly with a string hanging inside. After several days, large sugar crystals coat the string. What process formed the crystals?

options.text: Filtration, because the string acted like a filter trapping sugar from the water
Distillation, because the water evaporated leaving the sugar on the string behind
Crystallization, because cooling reduced solubility and sugar formed solid crystals
Decomposition, because the sugar broke down into simpler substances on the string

quiz_questions.explanation: This is crystallization. The hot saturated sugar solution became supersaturated as it cooled, and excess sugar came out of solution as solid crystals that grew on the string.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemist wants to purify a sample of copper sulfate crystals that contain some sand impurities. What sequence of steps should they follow?

options.text: Dissolve in hot water, filter out the sand, then cool the solution to crystallize pure copper sulfate
Heat the mixture until it melts, pour off the liquid copper sulfate, and let it cool into crystals
Use a magnet to separate the copper sulfate from the sand, then wash with cold water to clean
Use chromatography to separate copper sulfate and sand, then collect the copper sulfate spots

quiz_questions.explanation: This is recrystallization: dissolve the impure crystals in hot water, filter out the insoluble sand, then cool the filtrate to produce pure copper sulfate crystals.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a pharmaceutical lab, a scientist needs to separate a medicine crystal from a liquid mixture. The crystals are too fine to settle on their own. What technique should they use first?

options.text: Distillation to evaporate the liquid and leave the crystals behind in the flask
Vacuum filtration to quickly pull the liquid through a filter and collect the crystals
Chromatography to separate the dissolved medicine from the liquid on paper strips
Decanting to pour off the liquid carefully while the crystals float on the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Vacuum filtration uses reduced pressure to pull the liquid through filter paper faster and more efficiently, making it ideal for collecting fine crystals that are suspended in liquid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student rapidly cools a hot sugar solution by placing it in an ice bath. They get many tiny crystals instead of a few large ones. Why?

options.text: Rapid cooling produces many small crystals because particles have less time to organize
The ice bath chemically reacted with the sugar and broke it into smaller pieces overall
The container was too small so the crystals could not grow to a full large size in it
The student used too much water in the solution, which prevented large crystal growth

quiz_questions.explanation: Rapid cooling causes many crystal nuclei to form simultaneously, but each has little time to grow. Slow cooling allows fewer nuclei to form, and each grows into a larger, more organized crystal.

quiz_questions.question_text: A water treatment plant uses large sand filters to clean water. Why is sand an effective filter material?

options.text: Sand chemically reacts with and destroys bacteria and viruses in the water supply
Sand grains have small spaces between them that trap particles as water flows through
Sand absorbs all chemicals dissolved in water and converts them to harmless substances
Sand is magnetic and attracts impurities out of the water as it passes over the grains

quiz_questions.explanation: The small spaces between sand grains act as tiny filters, trapping suspended solid particles, debris, and some microorganisms as water passes through the bed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geologist finds a cave with large quartz crystals. These crystals formed naturally over thousands of years. What conditions most likely helped them grow so large?

options.text: Very rapid cooling of mineral-rich water trapped in the cave underground
Very slow cooling over a long time with a steady supply of dissolved minerals
Extremely high pressure that compressed tiny crystals into larger single ones
Volcanic lava flowing through the cave and melting the rock into crystal shapes

quiz_questions.explanation: Large natural crystals form when mineral-rich solutions cool extremely slowly over long periods. This gives particles time to arrange into highly organized, large crystal structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tries to filter a saltwater solution to separate the salt from the water. The filtrate is still salty. Why did filtration not work?

options.text: The filter paper had holes that were too large and let the salt through easily
Salt is dissolved in water, so its particles are too small for the filter to trap
The student did not pour the solution slowly enough through the filter paper
Saltwater cannot be separated by any method because it is a chemical compound

quiz_questions.explanation: Dissolved salt particles are at the molecular level — far too small for filter paper to trap. Filtration only works for insoluble solids. To recover salt, evaporation or crystallization is needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student drops a small crystal of potassium permanganate into a supersaturated solution. Suddenly, purple crystals form rapidly throughout the solution. What role did the small crystal play?

options.text: It chemically reacted with the solution and created new purple crystal compounds
It acted as a seed crystal that triggered crystallization of the excess dissolved solute
It dyed the solution purple and the color change caused the solute to solidify quickly
It lowered the temperature of the solution dramatically which forced all the crystals out

quiz_questions.explanation: The small crystal acted as a seed crystal. In a supersaturated solution, introducing a seed crystal provides a surface for excess dissolved solute to begin crystallizing rapidly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coffee maker uses a paper filter to brew coffee. The dark liquid passes through while the grounds stay in the filter. A student says this is the same principle as lab filtration. Are they correct?

options.text: No, coffee brewing is a chemical reaction and lab filtration is only physical separation
No, coffee filters work by evaporation while lab filters work by trapping solid particles
Yes, both use a porous barrier to separate insoluble solid particles from a liquid
Yes, but only because coffee is technically considered a chemical compound not a mixture

quiz_questions.explanation: The student is correct. Coffee brewing uses filtration — hot water extracts soluble compounds from the grounds, and the paper filter traps the insoluble grounds while letting the brewed coffee through.
